raw_case:
Fibromuscular dysplasia: An underrated cause of chronic kidney disease in developing countries: A rare case report
Renal fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) is one of the rare cardiovascular conditions affecting the kidneys at very young ages. The exact pathophysiology is still not known and is one of the causes of resistant hypertension in young patients. Severe forms of FMD such as those involving bilateral renal arteries are very few reported in the literature. In this case, we report a severe form of FMD resulting in the rapid progression of chronic kidney diseases in a young patient which results in requirement of renal replacement therapy.

Introduction

Fibromuscular dysplasia is a rare form of systemic vascular disease that affects the young population, the majority being women, and accounts for more than 10% of all renal artery stenosis [1]. The prevalence is estimated to be 12 per 100,000 individuals as per a review done in 2021 by Rana et al. [1]. The most affected artery is the right renal artery with approximately (60%-75%) of all cases, although this can happen elsewhere involving major vessels such as the carotid and vertebrae arteries [2]. Hypertension secondary to FMD is the most common clinical presentation in young patients, particularly females. Herein we present a case of FMD which resulted in rapid progression to chronic kidney diseases in a young female of African descent with involvement of both the right and left renal arteries.

Case presentation

A 29-year-old female patient of African descent presented to the outpatient clinic with a complaint of nausea accompanied by loss of appetite for the past 1 week. These symptoms were preceded by on-and-off headaches and easy body fatigue a month before presentation. She has a positive history of hypertensive heart disease in first-degree relatives of the family including her parents. She does not report any history of other chronic illnesses but reports being kept on anti-hypertensives a year ago but had poor adherence to drugs. On examination pale, with mild lower limb swelling, on auscultation decreased air entry in the right infra-mammary region other examination findings were unremarkable. Her blood pressure was 198/110 mmhg, heart rate 88 bpm. Laboratory investigations showed creatinine of 2000 micromols/l (60-120 micromols/l), urea of 45 micromols/l (2.5-6.5 mmol/l), hemoglobin count (Hb) 6.9 g/dL(12-16 g/dL), autoimmune profile including antinuclear antibody, cyclic citrullinated peptide, and serum metanephrine were unremarkable. Results showed severe anemia with poorly differentiated corticomedullary differentiation Fig. 1. Contrasted abdominal-pelvis Computed tomography was ordered to assess for any structural abnormalities of the kidneys and surrounding vessels, which showed chronic kidney disease with bilateral renal artery beading Fig. 2. Fig. 3 shows a cross-sectional view of the right and left affected renal arteries. The patient was started on hemodialysis as was not fit for other modalities of treatments due to the kidney loss.

Discussion

Fibromuscular dysplasia involving the renal artery presents with symptoms of hypertension, which will include elevated blood pressure, headache, dizziness, and occasionally tinnitus [3]. These patients are known to have a long-standing history of elevated blood pressure readings which are extremely high and very alarming [2,4]. Currently, literature has shown the physiology of FMD leading to chronic hypertension, but still sporadic cases or none have been reported to the best of our knowledge which have complicated to chronic kidney disease in the second decade of life.The diagnosis of FMD is complicated and involves the clinician to have a high index of suspicion together with history of illness and the clinical presentation of the patient [5]. Currently, imaging remains one of the most reliable investigation which uses CT- angiography to study renal blood flow [6]. Other superior investigations like magnetic resonance imaging are used, but due to availability and expensiveness contrasted computed angiography remains the gold standard for the diagnosis of FMD of the kidney. Contrasted CT-angiography will show a sign called “string of beads” which is diagnostic for this condition, such as seen by our patient.The treatment is aimed at the following goals, control patients' blood pressure, preservation of renal function, and prevention of complications resulting from this [2]. Three modalities of treatment have been used to date which include medical management, percutaneous intervention (angioplasty and stenting), and surgical revascularization [2,5,7]. Medical therapy with the use of anti-hypertensives like ACEI inhibitors has been seen as the primary modality of treatment before other advanced modalities [2]. Although patient compliance with the use of these antihypertensives regularly has been seen to be poor [2]. Revascularization is indicated in patients with hemodynamically significant renal artery stenosis, such as bilateral stenosis and resistant hypertension on medical treatment [8]. Conventional balloon angioplasty is the therapy indicated for renal artery stenosis caused by FMD with a cure or improved hypertension in 60%-90% of cases [8,9]. Despite all these, literature shows that the nature of progression of the disease has minimal effects on renal function hence pharmacotherapy is the initial treatment of choice for patients with FMD [9]. Our patient had resistant hypertension even though being on medications that were poorly adhered to, she rapidly developed a severe form of bilateral FMD which resulted in chronic kidney disease requiring renal replacement therapy.

Conclusion

In resource-limited settings, there is lack of diagnostic tools for these challenging medical conditions. We have seen a case of severe FMD at a young age leading to a significant morbidity to the patient and affecting her quality of life at a younger age. This condition is still rare and its exact pathophysiology is not known, clinicians in resource-limited settings should always screen for hypertension in patients at young ages as still possess the risk of having such cardiovascular emergencies. Early diagnosis is life-saving and will prevent social-economic effects on the patients.

Patient consent

The authors have obtained written informed consent for publication from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 29-year-old female of African descent.
- **Chief Complaint**: Nausea and loss of appetite for the past week.
- **History of Present Illness**: On-and-off headaches and easy fatigue for a month preceding the complaint. Noted poor adherence to antihypertensives prescribed a year prior.
- **Past Medical History**: Hypertension; poor compliance with medication. No other chronic illness reported.
- **Personal History**: None.
- **Family History**: Positive for hypertensive heart disease in first-degree relatives, including parents.
- **Physical Examination**: Pale, mild lower limb swelling, decreased air entry in the right infra-mammary region. Blood pressure: 198/110 mmHg; heart rate: 88 bpm. Other findings unremarkable.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
   - Creatinine: 2000 µmol/L (reference: 60-120 µmol/L)
   - Urea: 45 mmol/L (reference: 2.5-6.5 mmol/L)
   - Hemoglobin: 6.9 g/dL (reference: 12-16 g/dL)
   - Autoimmune profile (ANA, cyclic citrullinated peptide) and serum metanephrine: Unremarkable.
   - Imaging: Abdominal and pelvic CT revealed chronic kidney disease with bilateral renal artery "string of beads" appearance, diagnostic of fibromuscular dysplasia.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)**:
   - Supported by markedly elevated creatinine, urea, and chronic hypertension.
   - Confirmed by imaging showing poorly differentiated corticomedullary differentiation.

2. **Fibromuscular Dysplasia (FMD)**:
   - Considered due to unexplained resistant hypertension in a young woman, systemic symptoms (headache, fatigue), and family history of hypertension.
   - Confirmed by CT findings of bilateral renal artery "string of beads," typical of FMD.

3. **Other Potential Causes of Renal Artery Stenosis**:
   - Atherosclerotic disease: Unlikely in a patient this young with no comorbidities like diabetes, obesity, or hyperlipidemia.
   - Vasculitis or autoimmune diseases (e.g., Takayasu arteritis): Ruled out with unremarkable autoimmune profile.

4. **Secondary Hypertension Etiologies**:
   - Endocrine causes, such as pheochromocytoma or primary hyperaldosteronism, were excluded with normal serum metanephrine and clinical presentation.
   - Polycystic Kidney Disease ruled out by negative imaging findings.

5. **Severe Anemia**:
   - Likely multifactorial, secondary to chronic kidney disease and poor nutritional intake.

FMD was the most probable cause of the patient’s resistant hypertension and CKD based on imaging findings and clinical presentation. Other causes were systematically excluded through labs, history, and imaging results.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Severe fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) of bilateral renal arteries resulting in resistant hypertension and rapid progression to chronic kidney disease requiring renal replacement therapy.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Severe bilateral renal artery fibromuscular dysplasia leading to chronic kidney disease.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 29-year-old African American woman presenting with nausea and loss of appetite for a week. She's had headaches and fatigue for a month before that. She has a history of hypertension but hasn't been taking her meds regularly. Family history of hypertensive heart disease.

First, looking at her vital signs: BP is sky-high at 198/110. That's a hypertensive crisis. But why? She's young, so secondary hypertension is more likely than primary. Also, her poor adherence to antihypertensives might explain some of the BP issues, but the elevated creatinine and urea point to kidney problems. Creatinine at 2000 µmol/L is way above normal—severe renal impairment. Urea is also super high. Her hemoglobin is 6.9, which is anemia, probably due to chronic kidney disease since the kidneys produce erythropoietin.

Physical exam shows pale (anemia), lower limb edema (could be from nephrotic syndrome or heart failure), and decreased air entry in the right infra-mammary area. Wait, that's the lung. Maybe a pleural effusion? Could be related to fluid overload from kidney failure.

The CT scan shows chronic kidney disease with "string of beads" in the renal arteries, which is classic for fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD). FMD often causes renal artery stenosis, leading to hypertension. So, in her case, the hypertension could be secondary to FMD. Poorly controlled hypertension can lead to hypertensive nephropathy, accelerating kidney damage. But here, the renal artery stenosis from FMD would reduce blood flow to the kidneys, activating the renin-angiotensin system, causing even higher BP. This vicious cycle could lead to chronic kidney disease and eventually end-stage renal disease.

Her non-adherence to meds probably exacerbated the hypertension, worsening renal function. The anemia is likely due to CKD (erythropoietin deficiency). The nausea and loss of appetite are symptoms of uremia from kidney failure. The headaches and fatigue are probably from the severe hypertension.

Other tests like autoimmune profiles and metanephrines were normal, ruling out autoimmune diseases like lupus nephritis and pheochromocytoma. So, the main issue here is fibromuscular dysplasia causing renal artery stenosis, leading to secondary hypertension, which progressed to hypertensive nephropathy and chronic kidney disease (likely stage 5 given the creatinine level). The end result is end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis. The edema and pleural effusion are signs of fluid overload from kidney failure.

So the final diagnosis combines the underlying fibromuscular dysplasia with the resulting complications: chronic kidney disease due to renal artery stenosis from FMD, leading to ESRD, and secondary hypertension. The anemia and uremia symptoms are all part of CKD complications.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s markedly elevated creatinine (2000 µmol/L) and urea (45 mmol/L) indicate severe renal failure, consistent with chronic kidney disease (CKD). The anemia (Hb 6.9 g/dL) is likely secondary to CKD due to reduced erythropoietin production.

<step 2> Hypertension (198/110 mmHg) is a critical finding. The “string of beads” appearance on renal artery imaging confirms fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD), a known cause of renovascular hypertension. Poor adherence to antihypertensives likely exacerbated hypertensive damage, accelerating renal dysfunction.

<step 3> The combination of FMD-induced renal artery stenosis and uncontrolled hypertension creates a vicious cycle: renal ischemia from stenosis activates the renin-angiotensin system, worsening hypertension, which further damages renal parenchyma, leading to CKD progression.

<step 4> Symptoms (nausea, loss of appetite, headaches, fatigue) align with uremia and severe hypertension. Lower limb edema and decreased air entry suggest fluid overload and possible pleural effusion, common in advanced CKD.

<step 5> Exclusion of autoimmune causes (normal ANA, anti-CCP) and pheochromocytoma (normal metanephrines) supports FMD and hypertension as primary drivers of renal failure.

### Answer:
End-stage renal disease secondary to fibromuscular dysplasia-induced renovascular hypertension.
